author: @MartinPHellwig
text: If you get a lengthy NDA (well at least here in the UK), that also goes in length onto IP, have a quick glance through if it mentions anywhere specifically copyright (most don't), then sit back smile and say: You really want me to sign it? You do realise I will have all copyright on my written material? This will get enough confusion that you can suggest a much more reasonable NDA.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Martin P. Hellwig good one mate

author: @FredGandt

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i know what you mean mate

author: @Erdie5
text: NDAs are bogus.  I used to do custom applications with chemistry equipment, one company wanted me to sign an NDA that anything I did at their facility could not be replicated anywhere else.  I just laughed, how can you hire me as a consultant, then claim ownership of my knowledge?  In the end, they made an exception.  Be careful out there!

author: @TheBaconWizard
text: Oh yes!! My fave one effectively prevented me from working as a consultant for anyone else for 5 years. Goodbye business!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good god - exactly what i mean

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith It's those nasty terms like "nor any related activity" 

I do like highlighter pens.
